topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The envelope lay on the kitchen counter, a stark white rectangle mocking me. My hands were shaking so badly I had to grip the edge of the granite countertop to steady myself. Inside, the address was printed in neat, impersonal script: Denver, Colorado. My breath hitched, a sharp, shallow intake that did little to fill my lungs. Liam. My Liam. He was actually leaving. The realization hit me like a physical blow, stealing the air from my chest. I stared at the paper, the ink blurring into meaningless shapes. I didn't move for a full minute, just a low, guttural sound escaping my throat. Then, with a sudden, violent motion, I snatched the envelope, crumpling it into a tight, furious ball before hurling it against the wall. The sound echoed in the small apartment, sharp and ugly. I paced the living room, my steps quick and uneven, kicking at the rug as if trying to physically shake the news out of the air. Every thought was a jagged shard of resentment, aimed squarely at the distance that was suddenly expanding between us.

[story 2]

"You're serious?" The words tasted like ash in my mouth. I hadn't even let myself process the details; I’d just seen the confirmation email pop up on my phone. Across the screen, Liam’s smiling face was superimposed over a picture of a mountain range I’d once shared with him. My jaw clenched so tightly I could feel the muscles bunching beneath my skin. I dropped the phone onto the worn sofa cushion, the plastic thud sounding disproportionately loud in the quiet room. I stood up abruptly, my posture rigid, every line of my body screaming tension. I walked to the window, staring out at the familiar street, but the view felt alien, tainted by the impending separation. I pressed my forehead against the cool glass, letting out a slow, ragged exhale that held no warmth. When I turned back, my eyes were blazing, fixed on the empty space where Liam used to sit. I couldn't speak; the sheer volume of frustration threatened to choke me.

[story 3]

I reread the text message three times, each sentence landing with the weight of a stone. It was definitive. He was relocating. Across the country. A cold, hard knot tightened in my stomach, radiating outward until my hands started trembling uncontrollably. I slammed my laptop shut; the sound was explosive, a declaration of utter defeat. I stood in the center of the room, breathing in short, sharp gasps. My knuckles were white where I gripped the edge of the desk, the vibration of my own rising agitation almost audible. I began to pace in tight, angry circles, my gaze sweeping over every object in the room as if searching for a flaw, a reason, anything to justify this seismic shift. "No," I whispered, the sound barely audible, laced with a brittle edge. "No, you can't just do this." The air around me felt thick, charged with a volatile energy I desperately wanted to expel.
